name: TLA+ Specifications as IRs
summary: TLA+ is a specification language describing concurrent and distributed system semantics. Specifications are state machines with explicit invariants, expressed in mathematical logic, queryable by the TLC model checker. Counterexample traces become navigable artifacts.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: TLA+ is a *specification language*, not an IR proper — it describes concurrent and distributed system semantics for human and tool reasoning, not for compilation. It is included here because the specifications themselves are representations: state machines with explicit invariants, expressed in mathematical logic, queryable by the TLC model checker.

sections.title: Specifications as queryable artifacts
sections.content:
The connection to IRs: TLC produces a finite-state model from the spec, and its outputs (state graphs, counterexample traces) are themselves representations the user navigates. TLA+ Toolbox's Trace Explorer treats counterexamples as interactive debuggable artifacts.

The lesson: a specification language can play an IR-like role for verification tools. The downstream consumers of a TLA+ spec — TLC, TLAPS, the Trace Explorer — all treat the spec as a structured representation rather than as documentation, and operate on it the same way a compiler operates on a CFG or AST.
